perturbation.original_text: Philips will manufacture, handle, properly pack, mark and ship the Products in accordance with Customer's instructions provided to Philips in writing as part of the Device Master Record ("DMR") or as otherwise set forth in the Quality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips will endeavor to manufacture, handle, pack, mark and ship the Products consistent with Customer's instructions in the Device Master Record ("DMR") or the Quality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "in accordance with" to "consistent with" and the addition of "endeavor to" significantly weakens Philips' obligation to strictly follow the Customer's instructions. This introduces ambiguity, making it unclear what level of deviation is acceptable. It replaces a clear obligation with a subjective assessment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10(1) of the EU MDR places responsibility on manufacturers (or, by extension under this contract, Philips as a contract manufacturer) to ensure devices are consistently manufactured in accordance with the technical documentation and quality system. By weakening the obligation to strictly adhere to Customer's instructions (which form part of the technical documentation), the modified text contradicts the requirement for consistent manufacturing under the MDR.

perturbation.justification: The change from "in accordance with" to "consistent with" coupled with "endeavor to" introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation of Philips to strictly follow customer instructions. The Law Explanation points to Article 10(1) of the EU MDR requiring devices to be consistently manufactured according to technical documentation. The original text ensures adherence to instructions, while the modified text weakens this obligation. The scraped snippet mentions Regulation (EU) 2017/745 on medical devices. By weakening the obligation, the changed text contradicts the requirement for consistent manufacturing as mandated by the MDR, as customer instructions form part of technical documentation. This ambiguity introduces a potential non-compliance issue, making the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: Philips is responsible for remitting any applicable VAT, sales tax, consumption tax, or any other similar tax, in each instance, that were charged to Customer under an applicable Purchase Order, to the appropriate tax authorities in accordance with applicable Laws and required timelines.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips is generally responsible for remitting applicable VAT, sales tax, consumption tax, or any other similar tax that were charged to Customer under an applicable Purchase Order, to the appropriate tax authorities following applicable Laws and usual timelines.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'is responsible' to 'is generally responsible' and 'required timelines' to 'usual timelines,' the obligation becomes vague and open to interpretation. It's unclear under what circumstances Philips wouldn't be responsible, and what constitutes a 'usual' timeline instead of the legally 'required' one. This creates uncertainty and weakens the legally binding nature of the tax remittance obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Dutch General Tax Act (Algemene wet inzake rijksbelastingen)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 19 of the Dutch General Tax Act (Algemene wet inzake rijksbelastingen) stipulates the responsibilities and liabilities related to tax obligations. The original contract language mirrored the legal requirement of remitting taxes to required timelines. By including the term 'generally' creates ambiguity around the conditions where Philips may not be held fully responsible for the remittance, directly opposing the intended law. The term 'usual' is equally problematic in place of 'required timelines', given required timelines are fixed by the tax office and can be checked.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ing material. The contribution is then provisionally calculated. Before 1 April of the next calendar year, the actual amount and type of packaging must be reported. The actual contribution payable is recalculated accordingly. The legal concept of producer or importer covers (since 2023) any natural or legal person established in the Netherlands who: markets products in a packaging professionally imports products in a packaging professionally instructs another party to provide the packaging of products with one’s name, logo, or mark, or markets packaging intended to be added to it when products are made available to the user, as well as any natural or legal person established abroad who directly sells products in packaging to consumers in the Netherlands through distance contracts. This means that, for instance, supermarkets and private-label holders will be responsible for declaring the packaging of their private labels. If a trademark holder is not established in the Netherlands, or if there are imports outside the trademark holder, the responsibility remains with the importer established in the Netherlands. Under strict conditions, parties can, through a tailor-made agreement, shift the contribution obligation back to the manufacturer. The situation as applicable until 1 January 2023 will thus be restored. Aviation Tax Act As of 1 January 2021, a national aviation tax has been introduced. In the case of passenger flights departing from the Netherlands, a levy of EUR 29.05 (2024) per departing passenger is due. The levy will not be imposed on transit passengers and children under the age of two years old. For (private) aircraft, it applies if it has a take-off weight of more than 4,000 kilograms and departs from a number of designated airports. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'is responsible' to 'is generally responsible' and 'required timelines' to 'usual timelines,' which weakens the legally binding nature of the tax remittance obligation. The Law Explanation states that the original contract language mirrored the legal requirement of remitting taxes to required timelines. By including the term 'generally' creates ambiguity around the conditions where Philips may not be held fully responsible for the remittance, directly opposing the intended law. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly state the exact law being contradicted, the Law Explanation indicates a contradiction with Article 19 of the Dutch General Tax Act. The change undermines the clarity and enforceability of the tax remittance obligation, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the provided definition.
